title: Functional Programming and Data Structures using Scala
comment_content:
As the title suggests, this is a course on the general principles of programming languages (but we somehow ended up doing everything in Scala anyways....?)

The first half of the course is a in-depth dive into different parts of the Scala 3 language, with a focus on the functional aspect of it.
Topics of this part include:
Evaluation strategies (Call-by-name and Call-by-value)
Higher order functions (Functions returning functions and currying)
Immutability and Object-Oriented Programming in Scala (Abstract classes, immutable data structures such as Cons-Lists, and companion objects)
Common Functional Constructs (Map, Filter, Reduce, Folds, and Pattern Matches on case classes)
Subtyping and Variance (What is the type of a function or a method? When is a function a subtype of the other?)
Proofs on Immutable Data Structures (Lists and Trees, Structural Induction)
Maps (the Dict kind in Python) and For-Expressions (For-loops, but functionally)
Monads, Monoids, and Higher-Kinded Types
Lazy evaluation (Lazy lists and infinite sequences)
Context management in Scala (the using and given constructs)
State and Referential Transparency

The second half of the course is a whirlwind tour of other programming paradigms, but still grounded in Scala because that is the language we use to implement most of these paradigms (and definitely *not* because LP likes Scala):
Reactive Programming (Observers and Functional Reactive Programming)
Lisp and Scheme (Both writing programs in Lisp and implementing a subset of Scheme using Scala are included)
Prolog (Logic programming, matching and unification)
Parallel Programming (Futures and their composition, Async callbacks)
Actor Systems

The midterm focused on pattern matching and higher-order functions (which are going to be your bread-and-butter), subtyping, and structural induction.

The finals focused on pure functional programming (moving a imperative program to functional), Lisp (Writing and translating programs in Lisp with Lisp), the using construct, and functional representations of booleans (Church booleans).

The assignments and labs had a heavy focus on immutable data structures (to the point it was an inside joke of mine to refer to this course as Data Structures in Scala).

Overall, the course is *extremely* content rich, and certainly a must-take if you're interested in functional programming and/or interested in learning more about the listed topics.

Also fair warning: This course is quite hard.

(Don't take this course if you hate Scala).
comment_teaching:
Prof Parreaux (LP) taught quite well. He elaborated on the concepts presented quite nicely. The teaching materials were well-prepared, and had a few exercises for you to work out if you want extra practice. He was willing to answer questions or slow down his pace when we're confused about something (for example the topic on variance and subtyping). He (and his teaching team) was readily available to answer questions on the course Discord server, whether it be course concepts, assignment questions, or general chit-chat.

The labs were well-delivered and interactive (which is quite a rarity in HKUST). They were divided into exercise sessions (where a few of us formed groups and worked out pen-and-paper exercises related to the course material on the lab whiteboard), and the labs (which were less interactive (but no less interesting) and focused on individual completion of Scala coding tasks).

One nitpick about the lecture delivery is that the pace is quite fast - you'll end up confused even if you're trying to follow along. Spend some time clearing up the concepts and you'll be fine.
comment_grading:
Grading scheme is normal, per COMP course standards:
30% Assignments (10% each)
30% Midterm
40% Finals

Lab attendance is mandatory and will be taken, though not counted towards the final letter grade. No idea what happens if you miss one - though try not to since they are quite fun.

Assignments are surprisingly tricky and require a lot more thinking before writing any code - the assignments are like COMP 3711's in this regard. Assignment 1 was relatively easier (a n-ary tree), Assignment 2 was painful (a Rope data structure which had tons of special cases), and Assignment 3 (extending the Scheme interpreter to support objects, pattern matching, and call-by-need) required quite a bit of time in order to work out where even to begin. Plan your time accordingly.

Assignment Statistics:
Low/LQ/Mean/Median/UQ/High/Max:
1: 0/7.25/8.01/9/10/10/10
2: 2.8/7.25/7.91/7.8 9/10/10
3:0/0.7/5.53/6.2/9.2/10/10 (Note the huge difference between LQ and UQ)

Midterms and finals were acceptably difficult, and were a very nice learning experience for me - each exam managed to teach me something new (Using the type system in Scala to prove subtyping? Church booleans? Fun!... but quite scary in the exam, especially the subtyping question). The scores are distributed quite nicely, but beware that 1 mark in the exam = 0.75 or 1% of the course grade.

Exam statistics:
Low/LQ/Mean/Median/UQ/High/Max:
Midterm: 13.5/22.25/26.6/25.5/32/39.5/40
Finals: 9/21.5/26.45/28/31.25/39.5/40

(Note: it was impressive how quickly the finals were graded - it only took the TAs 6 hours from when the finals finished to release marks.)

~4 marks below the (estimated) UQ yielded an A-.
comment_workload:
If you only aim to do well in the course, then the workload is moderate - do the assignments a bit earlier, do the practice paper(s) that LP provides, and you should be good to go. Although the provided midterm prep questions were... not on the same difficulty level as the actual midterm, they did prepare us for the coverage of the midterm quite well. (Perhaps it's good to note that LP's exams can be.... interesting when he puts his mind to it).

Trying to grasp *all* the knowledge in the course, however, will drop the workload somewhere around a C or a D. Quite a bit of the course materials are not examined, and you can get a nice grasp on what would according to the sample papers. That means that there are actually lots of things that aren't examined, and some of them are still a mystery to me after the semester (the part of higher-kinded types, actor systems, and functional reactive programming is still confusing for me after the semester).

Unlike what I've read in the other course he teaches (COMP 4901U), the labs here aren't work-intensive. You show up, finish whatever is being asked of you, then you're good to go. Remember to sign the attendance sheet though.

author: 31v12
title: the most polarised review section i have ever seen
comment_content:
My god it feels like a cult when reading these reviews.

I want to remind everyone who is thinking about taking this course: usually reviews are pretty polarised, either they are really happy with the course or they wanna flex their grades so they write a review to compliment, or because they hate the course so much so that they need to express their anger; in many cases, they will be biased, thus it is very hard to judge whether a good course in good or not.

Take for example comp 3311, which is universially hated by everyone because the professor sucks and the slides suck; but after taking it, I don't believe that is the case here. While the professor does look like sleepy joe, he still puts effort into expressing new ideas just like a normal human would, and while the layout of the slides are legit ugly, the content is actually quite fulfilling that they are really good for self study.

What I'm trying to say is, a single review can't and will never show the full picture. Even though I will try to be as objective as possible, I will indirectly say something biased thus leading to anger to those who genuinely love the course. For those who taken have this already, please don't dislike this review because you disagree, instead leave a comment and have a valuable discussion.

Alright let's actually talk about comp 3031.

Lecture 1: Introduction & Scala Basics
Lecture 2: Tail Recursion & Function Values
Lecture 3: Classes 1
Lecture 4: Classes 2
Lecture 5: Bounds, Variance, Lists
Lecture 6: Tuples, More on lists
Lecture 7: Reasoning about lists, Maps
Lecture 8: Comprehensions, combinatorial search
Lecture 9: Queries, Random Generators
Lecture 10: Monads, Structural induction on trees
Lecture 11: Lazy Evaluation
Lecture 12: Type-directed Computation, Contexts and Type Classes 1
Lecture 13: Contexts and Type Classes 2
Lecture 14: State, Application Example
Lecture 15: Reactive Programming
Lecture 16: Lisp & Scheme
Lecture 17: Interpreting Scheme
Lecture 18: Logic programming 1
Lecture 19: Logic programming 2
Lecture 20: Concurrent and Reactive Programming (1/2)
Lecture 21: Concurrent and Reactive Programming (2/2)

This might be one of the most fulfilling course I have ever seen, there is just so much content covered that I think if you have coded with Scala before I think you will really enjoy this course by learning the fundementals of a programming language itself. And that's the problem, you need to have Scala experience in order to enjoy this. Unless you're a genius and/or you have a lot of time (I don't, I have 19 credits this semester and I'm terrible at time management), you will be overwhelmed by the sheer amount of stuff you need to have deep understanding of.

In some sense, even though they're mixed together, you can think of this course as 2 parts, Scala and Programming Principles, just like comp 2011 and comp 2012 with C++ and OOP. So this is an issue isn't it? The course is labelled as a 3 credit course even though you're basically taking comp 2012h but in Scala. Sure the workload in lower (more on that later), but there is a legit a language gap (no pun intented) when learning the content, which is why I think a lot of people are frustrated with the course; for me, because a lot concepts are taught using Scala, it feels like I'm learning math using Japanese while learning Japanese at the same time.

Looking back, I don't think the content is bad, but I think the course needs to have an overhaul to adjust to students who don't have a strong background in non-imperative programming languages, especially when the prerequiste is literally C++. Think like this, I mostly play osu!, so even though I'm very good at aiming, I am extremely bad at playing CS2 because I cannot convert those mouse skills when playing matchmaking and I don't have the basics of FPS games down. It just wouldn't make sense to play mm without even knowing the basics of controlling a gun.

So would breaking this course into 2 courses be better? Maybe? While learning Scala separately would certainly close the gap, I certainly understand that this would waste a lot of time for some who wants to take comp 4121 in spring. What about just skip Scala and use Python/C++ to learn programming paradigms? Again maybe..., but this would make a lot of changes to the course though (and the professor hates C++ apparently). I will let the instructor solve this problem.

Pattern matching changed my life btw
comment_teaching:
Man.

To be honest I can't give a full perspective on this because I have to run to LSK right after the lecture is finished, so I never really had a chance to have a chat with him, but my god is his accent heavy as fuck.

It doesn't help with the fact that he is speedrunning the slides everytime as they're 70 pages on average so it's legit really hard to understand what he's saying at first.

One thing comp 2011/2012 does well is that there are live demonstration of C++, so you can at least feel how they work without putting too much time by yourself, but he just doesn't do that, just reads the slides and maybe repeats things once or twice. At least he posts the zoom recordings so I won't give him an E.

So why teaching is C then? Because TA Anto Chen is fucking awesome. In labs (more details later), I was asking him about some bugs with the program, and not only does he helped me with the issue quickly, he also puts in the extra efforts to give me advices on other issues with the code. He is also genuinely is a great teacher, there was a lab I didn't understand anytime at all, and he just explains everytime in detail but being extremely friendly at the same time. I'm rooting for him to be an instructor for this course.
comment_grading:
Homework 30%
Midterm 30%
Final 40%

My score:
Homework 1: 9/10
Homework 2: 9.25/10
Homework 3: 8/10
Midterm: 17.5/30 (mean: 18.81)
Final: 18.5/40 (mean: 21.93)

Grade: C+

Very fair honestly, I was barely above LQ getting C+ is just expected at this point.

The funny thing was Midterm used to be 40 marks full marks, but because it was too hard compared to previous years, one of the questions was considered as bonus marks and for those who somehow gotten over 30 marks (:worship_q:), those extra points would be carried over to finals. Basically trying to make everyone have better grades cuz apparently this course is absolutely grading?

Speaking of difficulty of the exams, while both are 2 hours and there's not a lot content, it requires a lot of thinking and precise answers that is simply so difficult when you are not fluent in Scala. This is why it's hard for me to enjoy this course, I've used so much time translating Scala that I don't have time to think properly, it's just unfortunate.
comment_workload:
As a 3000 level course, around B.

The 3 homeworks are all programming assigments, with all 3 trying to applying concepts taught in class.

PA1: making trees using pattern matching, not bad but because I don't have experience in Scala, it took me almost a week of pain and suffering to finish it.

PA2: making tic tac toe game and somehow using comp 2211 stuff (minmax and pruning) and lazy evaluation to create a ttt AI. Took 2 days as I was slightly more comfortable in Scala.

PA3: modifying Lisp Interpreter to add extra functions. Again almost a week of pain and suffering because again, I lack fluency in Scala so I didn't know what to do until :worship_q: taught me how to do it.

One more thing: labs/exercise sections are mandatory but they don't count for anything, but holy shit are they hard af. My advice is really take this time to ask TA on ANY question, and you might just able to enjoy this course.

Edit:
Just a quick note here, by B I mean PASSING, if you wanna get A range, you will need need quite a lot more time self studying, making this D or even E

author: HTGAzureX1212
title: C++ is an unreasonable language.
comment_content:
The course goes over mainly functional programming and some data structures, alongside with some other programming paradigms over the end of the semester generally using Scala.

The first half of the semester focuses on functional programming with an in-depth dive into various parts of the Scala programming language. Refer to 151044's review for the exact topics (the list of topics here is a LazyList.)

The midterm was rather unconventional this semester, but it was surely interesting. As usual, it has a focus on (higher-order) functions, subtyping and variance, pattern matching. There's also a question on laziness and I had much fun figuring out the one-liners :P

Hasn't had the final exam yet and will update this review when that happens.

The course has much richer content than others, but the examinations don't really cover everything (not enough space...). Monads (simply monoids in the category of endofunctors) were not tested this midterm.
comment_teaching:
Professor Parreaux teaches quite well! The concepts covered in class are elaborated nicely, the materials were well-prepared and he answers questions. A 10-minute break is featured between two "sections" of the lecture :D

This year, the professor added examples of "implementation of similar concepts" found in the course in other programming languages like Haskell, OCaml, Rust, etc. which is quite interesting in my opinion. Notably, he left the slide on C++ empty because it is an unreasonable language.

The professor and the teaching team are readily available to answer questions in the Discord server, whether it was questions about the lectures, assignments or general chatting.

The labs are well delivered which is quite a rarity. They alternate between computer and pen-and-paper sessions. In the former you are given a small programming tasks in Scala to complete individually; while in the latter people form groups to work out exercises on a whiteboard (good practice for examinations honestly).

However, the pace of the lectures could be quite fast and may be hard to follow. Reviewing concepts after lecture is definitely a plus.

Oh, and, C++ is an unreasonable language (why am I taking 2012 with this ._.)
comment_grading:
Grading scheme is as follows:

30% programming assignments (10% each)
30% midterm examination
40% midterm examination

Lab (LA1) attendance is mandatory and will be taken.
There is another LA2 section for those who have documented time conflicts and watch the recordings of the labs instead.

Assignments are tricky yet interesting. Make sure to think before writing any code - don't yolo it. Assignment 1 was the implementation of a simple tree data structure. Assignment 2 was about tic-tac-toe and required implementing minimax and alpha-beta pruning (COMP2211 flashback moment). Assignment 3 was the Lisp dialect (Scheme--) interpreter, and required implementation of some additional features.

author: JimmyLeung1998
title: Good course, esp in the age of online learning
comment_content:
First of all, NO FINAL EXAMS DURING PANDEMIC. This totally turns the course into an assignment-based learning one, BUT, in programming assignment 2,3, we have some subsequent pop-up quizzes requiring us to give sketches on like parsing tree and execution flow, which is not too difficult as compared to the exams.

I think overall before starting on this course, you should have some background knowledge.
- data structure, c/c++ programming, hardcore recursion [COMP2012]
- relations, perhaps some algorithms (COMP2711/3711)
- scheduling, synchronization, threads (COMP3511 and/or COMP3021)
[..] refers to prerequisites, and (...) refers to prerequisites or corequisites. Interested students are strongly encouraged to study similar topics at the same time at different courses to gain more perspective.

Anyway in the following I would briefly talk about mainly the assignment topics, but not those which would only come up in the exam questions.

For conciseness I shall use the following header notation for each assignment topic.
  {{ number }}. {{ topic }} - {{ Language }}

1. Functional Programing - Sml
If you have previous experience in es6 nodejs or python functional programming, it would be easy for you. The only trouble is you would have to get into a old-school weird SML language in the assignment. This year the sml pa is associated with a in-class demo.

2. Program (Source code) parsing - Flex, Bison
This topic is about parsing a "statement" in a typical program. From simple examples like "1 + 2" to "1 + 2 * (3 - 4) / 5", you would be taught how to translate such statements, by setting up a set of proper interpretation rules (grammar) with appropriate syntax units and operators (tokens), together with the consideration on like associativity and precedence issue. Btw just in case you wanna get involved with more theoretical material about grammar, you could take another course COMP3721 which is also a fun one :) Assignment on this topic in this semester is also easy. This year the flex-bison pa is associated with a in-class quiz.

3. Logical Programming - Prolog
It is like SQL, but not exactly the same. In this topic you completely switch your mind from variables and functions into relations. So instead of "f(input) { return result; }", you would be thinking how to write "f(input, result) :- ...". Btw this is not supposed to be a difficult topic, I mean most people take this course as elective in year 3 or year 4, with probably their first course on discrete math on previous semester already. BUT, assignment in this year is extremely tough. And after two-week efforts we get another "simple" in-class quiz :)

4. Concurrent Programming - Cuda
As I am told, this topic in usual circumstance is taken as extra topic and hence would not appear in programming assignment typically. But here we are :) In this part you would trained into thinking in a way that, tasks on large array can be separated, so that each of your computer processors can share some workload, instead of one single program running one single large array data in a slow sequential manner. This is particularly similar to the idea of "map" in functional programming. I mean once you realize this, the labs / assignment somehow becomes easier for one to tackle. The programming assignment comes with a graded presentation.


Learning material:
SML: Chapter 1-9, 11 (about 100 pages) at http://www.cs.cmu.edu/~rwh/isml/
Flex-bison: First three chapters at https://web.iitd.ac.in/~sumeet/flex__bison.pdf
Prolog: Functional helper doc at https://www.swi-prolog.org/pldoc/man?section=libpl
Cuda: Official site at https://developer.nvidia.com/CUDA-zone
comment_teaching:
Lecture:
Good teaching even in online mode. But 9am lect cause me skipped most class :)

Lab:
Totally not helpful!! Do not hope to get a satisfied answer for the lab material from the TAs. But if you skip lectures a lot you may want to check the lab codes when doing assignment to (re)learn the stuff you need.

comment_workload:
Quite heavy. In case you are taking it in online semester, plz be ready for the assignment workload. But absolute grading + manageable assignment does help your grade in case you can work very hard.

Btw even for the toughest assignment (in this semester is prolog), there would still be students getting full marks on the programming assignment :) So if you have friends, you may wanna take the course with them, and help each other more on the assignment problems, given that the course is having absolute grading. DISCLAIMER: No direct cheating or plagiarism is encouraged here.

Prof is often willing to extend deadline for assignments, so in a way weaker students are still somewhat able to get high marks.

One thing is, you need to use cse remote linux to do the assignment (esp the cuda's one), and school devices (even in a virtual sense) are as always not that efficient :) I would suggest interested students should pick up some unix terminal skills and have their filezilla ready before rushing into this course. Otherwise you would be literally on fire when you choose to rush for your assignment with sloppy file syncing skills btw local and remote machine.

(For sure for remote file syncing, you can always use terminal tools like scp or rsync, but I am told that COMP3511 students are taught with slow filezilla so ...)

author: Sahab
title: Take this course after 3711
comment_content:
For the actual content, please refer to the other reviews. These are my opinion on the contents:

Overall, the course focused too much on "compilation" of the languages (at least for my liking), rather than important concepts in those paradigms that we would actually apply irl. Quoting another review, if you don't want to be a human compiler, do not take this course.

1. Functional Programming (SML)

On the choice of the language, SML is quite an old, I would even say outdated, language. There are more prominent purely functional programming languages out there such as Haskell which have much more use then SML.

As an intro to functional programming, the course content focused too much on syntax and how the interpreter works (type inference) rather than important FP concepts like partially applied functions and currying. Instead, the only FP concept I have learnt (which is already known by all CS student) is recursion.

However, it still managed to cover popular FP functions such as map and filter, so I would not regard this part as completely useless.

2. Grammars and Expressions (Flex and Bison)

IMO easier part of the course, may be (or may not be) useful in programming projects. Check Github and you will find some projects using Flex and Bison, tho not a lot of them.

3. Logical Programming (Prolog)

Again, a lot of syntax and inner workings of the interpreter (prolog search tree). My main complain is that the course material failed to capture the different mindset in prolog from C++ and even Functional programming. By focusing on syntax, it is hard for an average student like me to catch up with how think in a way that helps me to code in prolog. The low average score of PA2 clearly reflected this failure. Prolog is a beautiful language, and this course did not do it justice.

4. Procedural Programming (parameter passing and scoping)

Purely conceptual, little to no coding. This part teaches parameter passing methods that are more niche. May use these concepts in some languages, but not a lot of them.

5. Parallel Programming (CUDA)

This is the part I found the most fun in learning. An intro to parallel programming in CUDA, multi-thread programming for the GPU. Because the GPU has much more threads than the CPU, it is more suited for parallel programming, and CUDA is the more popular one for such a use. The lecture mainly used matrix computation as examples, which is quite clear and illustrative.

All in all, if I could turn back time, I would not have taken this course.
comment_teaching: Prof. Luo is very friendly, willing to answer questions. But expect a lot of PowerPoint reading from Prof and especially the TA in lecture and labs. Lab materials alone were enough for me to understand all course materials and I stopped attending class two lectures into the semester. But after all, I didn't get A-range so the secret may lie in lectures (?) That's for you to find out.
comment_grading:
Should be absolute grading. Grading is quite fair.
Ovr: 76 => B

A few tips for assignments:

Read past paper for code examples if you have time. Especially for Prolog. The codes in the past papers over the years covered different ways of SML and Proglog in tackling different problems, so you may want to look into them for your inspiration in assignments.

Another observation is this semester's assignment questions can mainly be viewed as graph questions. In particular, we are given a list of flights from between cities and need to find whether a path between two cities exists, what is the shortest path etc. Assignment 1 and 2 are basically the same, just in different programming languages. It may be worth your time to look into graph algorithms such as depth-first-search and Dijkstra before working on the assignment. Or, even better, take COMP3711 before taking this course.

Exams:

Just when I thought every question would follow past exam papers, this year's BNF and Parse Tree problem in the midterm exam includes recursion, not seen in other year's paper. Caught me off guard. And the Prolog search tree problem was also more difficult than before. So, although the other reviews may tell you to practice past exam papers, do prepare for the unexpected.
comment_workload:
3 programming assignments, each took me half a week to complete (or give up), so you may want to spare some time for each of them. Some assignment has 10 test-cases for grading, each correspond to 1 mark of the assignment, and each mark of the assignment correspond to 1 mark of your total grade. The examples given in the assignment document is NOT enough for you to pass all test cases, as I have learned painfully.

Stackoverflow doesn't help much either. SML and Prolog are not popular languages, so you are pretty much on your own. I did manage to find some solutions on SO but they are not sufficient in passing all test cases.

The prolog assignment is especially difficult, so please spare a lot of time for it and read some past exam papers to get inspiration for you codes.

author: Kelvin Y
title: Learn new programming languages and techniques
comment_content:
You basically learn a bunch of different programming languages. It requires you to master recursion for functional programming... I don't really know how to explain the others but basically you learn different types of programming.

I find the course content useful but difficult. If you manage to get a grasp of each language then you can do really well. It's more about understanding the syntax of each language.
comment_teaching:
She is boring and reads from the lecture notes. I fell asleep without fail every single lesson.

When she tries to give examples she sometimes messes up and is pretty slow with them. I'm sure she understands her stuff but she makes mistakes sometimes. We're all human, but she seems to make more mistakes than other professors I've seen.

Ask the TAs in the labs for help if you don't understand anything. Don't be shy.
comment_grading:
I got a few marks below mean in the midterm (out of 30) but full marks in the final (it was the semester that suddenly switched to online). I really studied hard for the final and made sure I understood all the languages.

Got an A in the end, felt nice to redeem myself.
comment_workload:
3 assignments + midterm + final

Assignments are based on the language you were taught. So make sure you understand the language's syntax. It can take some time so make sure you read the lecture notes and understand what each page says.

Make sure you don't make mistakes in your exams. It can get tricky. Redo each question like 5 times to make sure you got it right.

I guess it's a reasonable workload for a programming course.

author: Kokomi
comment_content:
Very fruitful and rich content. We learned a brand new topic almost every week. Many other reviews have listed all topics, so I will not repeat them. Generally speaking, the course teach you Scala and a lot of magical ways to write code. From my perspective, the content is very interesting. It covers many interesting ideas that you can see in modern programming languages. They can be completely different from Python and C++, so it can be challenging if you only have experience in those two programming languages.

Some people might think the content is useless. I admit that a small part of the content, such as logic programming, is like art. It is actually beautiful, but only a small number of people can admire it, and it cannot help you make money. However, a large proportion of the content is useful in the sense that it can be migrated to other programming languages or tasks. For example, you can see pattern matching in many other languages (and potentially even C++ in the future!). Also, observer and signal models are widely applied in frontend frameworks. In summary, if you view the content from a wider perspective, not just from Scala, you may find it is even more useful than you think.
comment_teaching:
C for lectures. I have to say LP does not teach very well although he is a good person. I feel like he assumes us to have decent knowledge in programming languages, and does not explain everything in a way that most of the students can understand. For a student who does have some prior knowledge, the lectures may be enjoyable. However, if some student does not have any background, they may feel they are not the right audience of the lectures and the teaching is too fast. In that case, attending lectures is probably not more helpful than reading the slides.

A+ for lab sections. I think this course has the best lab section design in all courses I have taken. The labs took place in one of the CS Labs. In half of the weeks, we are assigned some coding tasks and write actual Scala code. In the other weeks, we were divided into groups to complete written questions on the whiteboards. For both formats, the TAs would walk around the room to see how everyone is doing and whether someone need help. At the end of each section, they also explained the solution. The lab sections help me a lot in understanding the topics, practicing writing Scala code, and even preparing for exams. They are very interactive and meaningful, which is extremely rare in the department. Especially, I would like to worship Anto Chen here for his fantastic teaching, patience and helpfulness.

Besides, the teaching team created a discord server for us to ask questions and have discussions. The instructors and TAs are also reachable there, so the support we can get is more than sufficient. Recordings were also posted.
comment_grading:
- 30% PA (3 PAs in total, 10% each)
- 30% midterm
- 40% final

PAs are very interesting and reasonably challenging. I think they have top tier design among all COMP courses. The hardest one is PA3, which has a lot of small points that are easy to forget.

PA1 (Trees) : LQ=9 / Mean=9.31 / Median=10 / UQ=10 / High=10 | Me=10
PA2 (Tic-Tac-Toe) : LQ=9 / Mean=9.40 / Median=10 / UQ=10 / High=10 | Me=10
PA3 (Lisp Interpreter) : LQ=9 / Mean=9.23 / Median=10 / UQ=10 / High=10 | Me=9

I checked some reviews from previous semesters and I believe PA3 was the same as last year. However, the stats were much higher, so I really suspect that some students used AI (or answers from previous semesters) to complete the assignment.

Midterm and Final are kind of hard, but more manageable than 3711. The main challenge is the 2-hour time limit. I think the questions are fun and well designed. One or two of the questions can be found in the past papers provided by prof, so do check past papers. However, don't rely on past papers too much. For example, Lisp almost always appear in the finals of previous semesters but did not appear this time, but logic programming, which never appeared before, was included this time and made up a lot of points. A bad thing I would like to mention is that the exams only covers several topics in the course, while others are not tested at all (as I wrote in content section, we have a brand new topic almost once a week), which makes the exams not that comprehensive.

The grading of this semester is kind of puzzling. The stats of the midterm is so low compared to previous semesters that LP decided to turn a 10-point question into bonus, and the total marks of midterm changed from 40 to 30. They also gave some bonus points in the final, but I don't know how it worked. LP said the course uses criteria referencing instead of a curve to assign letter grades, so the changes in scores mentioned above would improve everyone's grade, although I don't fully understand how. Anyway, the stats below, if I understand it correctly, already include bonus, and the scores higher than full marks (30 in midterm and 40 in final) are normalized to full marks.

Midterm : LQ=11.75 / Mean=18.81 / Median=20 / UQ=26 / High=30 | Me=30 (originally 35.25)
Final : LQ=13.13 / Mean=21.93 / Median=22 / UQ=28.88 / High=40 | Me=40 (originally 43.5)

p.s. I met a lot of daai lou in this course, so do be careful >.<
comment_workload: There are only 3 PAs, so the workload from homework is not high. However, if you want to understand everything in the course, you need to put in a lot of time and effort. For me, the workload is higher than 3711 but lower than 4211.

author: rind
title: A must take course for programming lovers
comment_content:
This course teaches you different programming paradigm and more philosophical way of programming languages. It also check whether you are clever enough to learn other style of programming within one semester.

1. Functional Programming (SML)
Programming Language with only recursion, definitely nightmares for those who loves looping. As a strong typed PL with no memory for variables, SML eliminates all side effects that may occurs in procedural programming.

2. Grammar, Regular Expression (Flex and Bison)
The more philosophical way exploring the system of defining grammars, lexically analyse whether an input string belongs to the grammar (Parse tree, Regular Expression, CFG).
The second part consists of a practical use of defining your own grammar by:
Flex (Fast lexical Analyser): Analyse whether the input string matches the grammar.
Bison: Provide semantics for calculation.
(You have to write some C/C++)
With the above two tools, you can define your own grammar, and do whatever tricks you want to strings input (the PA of this chapter ask us to implement a program to play with the number of atoms in an organic chemical compound, which is super fun).

3. Logical Programming (Prolog)
At this point you have to completely switch your mindset, I would say functional programming is somehow similar to procedural programming (you call functions, execute line by line). However, Prolog presents a different way of thinking, instead of {input -> output}, Prolog put emphasis on relationship, which makes it powerful (I am still stunned by its powerful way of querying), but also extremely difficult to write.

4. Procedure Activations
Comp2011/2012 may cover some of it, mainly about how to call functions (by value, by reference..., which is related to lvalue and rvalue) and how activation records are created. Introduce C procedure in a more deep way.

5. Parallel and Concurrent Programming
Using Java as an example of parallel programming. As this course is teaching the "principles" only, so you don't have to know how to write java code. Also Comp3021(In Fall Semester) will have a more in-depth analysis on this topic.

6. GPU Parallel Programming (CUDA)
Very interesting topic but it is only a very brief introduction to CUDA programming. You can feel the power of parallel programming and it is an art to allocate recourses for each thread as performance can varies a lot. You have to use C/C++ to implement it.
comment_teaching:
Professor teaches quite well, and you can ask her in office hour if you have any problem with this course. Her tone is a little bit slow which makes me feel a bit sleepy in class.

Also, the content covered in slides are just the fundamentals of that topic. It is like teaching you 1+1 in class but ask you to perform differentiation in programming assignments.

One of the TAs, Chu Han response the emails super quick.
comment_grading:
Programming Assignments x 3 (10% each) on SML, Flex and Bison, and Prolog.
Midterm Exam 30%
Final Exam 40%

For me:
PA: 30/30
Midterm: 66/100 (mean: 60.8, high: 92, low: 28)
Final: 96/100 (mean: 79.4, sd:14.8, high: 100, low: 25)
Total: 88.2/100
Grade: A-
This course is adopting absolute grading, meaning 95 for A+, 90 for A, 85 for A-

The professor says she will adjust because the midterm is performing lower than expected and the assignment1 is relatively harder. I don't know whether she adjust it by making the final exam easier or what lol.

The midterm exam and final exam are quite similar to the past papers, do remember to do the past paper before the exam.

The midterm exam will ask you to write SML code from scratch, meaning that you must practice more.
The final exam provide skeleton code and you just fill in the blanks (one statement/ one predicate per blank), so you do not have to start everything from scratch and remember all syntax which is quite nice.

The TAs grade our assignments in person, meaning that there will be some mistakes in grading. If they find your code cannot be compiled, they will just give 0 marks. I receive 0 marks for once, appeal and finally get full marks.
comment_workload:
No attendance for class or labs.
The workload mainly comes from the assignments.
Assignment 1 is SML on graph theory, where you have some flights from City A to City B, then you have to implement a bunch of functions to find shortest paths, number of paths, etc.
Professor also thinks that Assignment 1 is more difficult this semester compared to previous semesters. Therefore start doing it earlier.
Assignment 2 is on Flex and Bison, like a breeze.
Assignment 3 is a nightmare, on Prolog, and I have never feel like I didn't make any progress after spending 3 days on this. The topic is same as the Flights one, but the hard part is prolog is using DFS for asking more answers, in order to obtain all possible answers without repeating, it is a hard work. I finally figure out a structure of writing code that finishes all 5 questions, and in total I spend 5 days on this.
But what is more important is that the sample answer consists of a bunch of functions not taught in class, meaning that this is also somehow a difficult task to implement using what the lecture slides taught us.
If you really struggle with prolog then you should ask for help earlier.

For the midterm and final, do more revision on past papers.

author: Tommy Li
title: The Art of Programming 🎨
comment_content:
This course gives an introduction to different programming paradigms (e.g. FP, LP) and some core concepts in programming language design (e.g. grammar and expression, scoping, parameter passing mechanism). For a programming nerd like me (see my COMP 2012H review lol), I truly enjoy the topics.

Part of the course is about alternative programming paradigms. You have to wrap you head around. The for loops are gone. They becomes functions and logical relations. How to write a program using functions only? You will be amazed by how weird but fun it is.

The other part is programming language design. Although you would not learn how to write a compiler in this course, you will learn some of the components of it. How would a compiler parse your program source file? How can the compiler determine which variable is which? Aside from the mechanisms, you can get a taste building your own little programming language without having to struggle to build a parser from scratch. Flex & Bison are pre-written parser/scanners that you will learn for implementing a language.

I always think programming is art. It feels so cool to code up your program in a special and neat way. This course can give your more inspirations on such possibilities. Cited from another review, "anyone loves programming should take this course".

But of course, don't take this course if you hate programming. lol
comment_teaching:
Luo's teaching is very clear and detailed. She goes over the materials in a good pace which an average student would be able to follow. She also supplements her teaching with lots of programming examples. Some of the cases are quite inspiring and would make you think more about the language/mechanism.

Lecture videos are also provided. They are extremely useful, especially if you cannot pay enough attention in morning classes or get lost during lecture time. However, I suggest attending the lectures because the screen is usually rendered poorly in the videos.

The TA reads out the lab materials word by word. Don't expect too much for the lab. Attending the lecture and trying out the materials by yourself should be sufficient.
comment_grading:
Mean => A-
:O

This course deploys absolute grading scheme. If you get every assignment above mean, you will get at least an A- (according to the stats). If you are aiming for A+, there is still some buffer for careless mistakes. The assignments and exams are in general not too difficult that you can perform reasonably good if you follow the course closely. Be careful tho as there are some minor tricky parts that need to be taken with care.

Grade mapping:
95 and above A+
90-94 A
85-89 A-
80-84 B+
75-79 B
70-74 B-
65-69 C+
60-64 C
55-59 C-
50-54 D
(Mapping is from official announcement)

Grading components:
assignment 1    20%
assignment 2     20%
assignment 3    20%
midterm exam    30%
online quiz     10%
(Please be noted that the weighting differs from previous offerings due to the special circumstance this semester)

Score stats and my score (for reference):
Assignment 1: 10/10 (mean: 9.4) => 20/20
Assignment 2: 10/10 (mean: 9.1) => 20/20
Assignment 3: 9.6/10 (mean: 8.5) => 19.2/20
Midterm: 96/100 (mean: 79.31, max: 96.00, sd: 15.60) => 28.8/30
Final: 9.5/10 (mean: 7.76, max: 10, sd: 2.39)
Overall: 97.5% => A+
comment_workload:
There are a total of three programming assignments in the course, and nothing more. Each assignment is only a few hours work if you follow the course closely. And, those are programming assignments but not Q&A boring copy & paste.

For preparing midterm and final, doing a few years of past papers should secure a reasonably good score. The length of midterm paper is about 1 hour. It won't cost you too much time for preparing the exams. Three years of past papers are provided in the course website (you don't even need to spend time petergao/coursehero!).

As mentioned, lecture videos are provided so you can learn according to your pace.

author: Dipsy
title: Anyone love programming should take this course, anyone hate being human compiler should not take this
comment_content:
This course is well structured and will teach 5 paradigms of programming languages, most of them you have never heard before, really can test whether you are a good programmer and capable to learn a totally different style of programming language, as everyone is at the same starting line.

1. functional programming (SML)
    programming language cannot use loops, all recursion.
2. gramma and expression, language parser
    (BNF notation + regex + C =  Flex and Bison)
3. logical programming (prolog)
    programming using predicates, again no loops.
4. procedure activation (C)
5. parallele and GPU programming (CUDA)

1,2,3 are big topics and the topic of the assignments.

midterm covers SML, BNF, and regex, final covers all.
Both exams are closed-book, closed-notes
the style is very similar to past paper, so doing more past paper can secure your grade
comment_teaching:
Professor teaches very well and has good English. She knows deeply about the topics that she taught, and can explain the details clearly, and when doing human compiler she never skip steps so everyone can understand fully. Her way to accomplish the questions taught in class is the way you can answer the questions in the exam, especially past paper review sections. So attend her lectures can bring you great benefit.

LAB is just repeating what is taught in lectures, only useful when giving tips on assignments
comment_grading:
assignment 30%, 10% each
midterm 30%
final 40%

Both exams are closed-book, closed-notes
the style is very similar to past paper, so doing more past paper can secure your grade

me assignmets 29.5/30, midterm 99/100, final 90.5/100, A+
comment_workload:
not high, all assignments can finish within 6 hours and 2 weeks is given for each assignment
most of my revision on exams is done in paper review in class

author: Yassine
comment_content: The course content is interesting. It provides a solid foundation in programming language concepts. The main challenge is Scala, it is a powerful but undeniably complex language to pick up. The shift to functional programming and its specific syntax can be a bit steep at first.
comment_teaching: The professor is clearly knowledgeable and knows the material inside out. However, the lectures can be a bit boring or dry at times. You’ll need to make a conscious effort to stay focused, as the delivery isn't always the most high-energy.
comment_grading: The grading structure is fair, and it's easy to get all assignments points, which are 30% of your final mark. Keep in mind that attendance at labs is mandatory, so make sure you show up to secure those points.
comment_workload: Overall, the workload is not bad at all. It’s very manageable as long as you stay on top of the programming tasks. The assignments are the only real "heavy lifters" in terms of time commitment. Otherwise, the course doesn't demand too much of your outside time.

author: Patient
title: Fruitful and Unnamable
comment_content:
Please do refer to others in the 2025-26 Fall; They are more or less the same.

And how come everything is in Scala3??????????????????

Personal Comment: Abundant, Fruitful, and Unnamable. I chose this course simply because I was (am always) the last group of ppl reg course (fkmylife), and it turned out to be a more enjoyable (still a bit suffering as always) course than 2211 and many other common core and-

Caution: This is not the place you want to be if you care too much about grades. If you care about the grade but not the content itself, you will enjoy this course in another intriguing way.

You will see many new concepts if it is your first 3xxx course (actually, most of the content is new to m,e which makes them both intriguing and suffocating)

Many gods in this course. Didn't know until I read the Discord messages (yea they have discord group, ask questions there)

Deference to everyone who made through this course yayyyy
comment_teaching:
Lab: Best lab in my life, colab lab with other ppl :D, friendly reachable TAs. (Attendance needed)

Lecture: not many ppl (<50%) attend the lecture, you can skip it and skim through the notes. I didn't dare to do that. Still could spend more time on examples for me to catch up ,_,

Agreed with another review, spending more time on examples will do a great favour for those who haven't touched similar topics
comment_grading:
Fair Grading: PL tried hard to give fair grades to those who were trying hard too.

For those who want to get a high grade in the exams, do not try to rely solely on the past papers; they are just for **Practice**, it doesn't necessarily mean that these questions will appear on it.

Reviewed the concept daily, played with codes, and ate a past paper 2 nights before the exam. So getting a B+ is reasonable for such amount of effort spent

Midterm: 25/30
Final: 25.5/40
PA: 10/10, 9/10, 9/10
Grade: B+
comment_workload:
Study: You'd better look at the official Scala3 doc (highly encouraged), don't even expect to grasp all the knowledge solely from the course material, yea and play with it. In short, use 1 hour to review per day

PAs: Fair workloads (Except the one I spent 6 hours debugging)

Time: I spend equally as much time as COMP3711 this sem.

Difficulty: Similar to COMP3711, manageable (except the variance part and prolog), sufficient Exam time.

author: Kwan00
title: Comments from a person who dropped the course during the add/drop period
comment_content:
=== I only attended two lectures, so here are some PERSONAL COMMENTS ===
(for reference, I am not a human compiler who love programming, but getting A range in COMP 2011 + 2012)

I would say I cannot understand the content even in the first two lectures. I agree that for those who are geniuses and full of interest, it is easy to follow, but for those who only want credit to graduate, think carefully before taking this course. The content is really hard for me, with long PowerPoint presentations, and your classmates are all geniuses.

I recommend sitting in on a lecture before enrolling in the course.
comment_teaching: I think he is nice when answering questions, but I don’t believe his explanations are sufficient for beginners because he seems to expect that you can understand everything in a very short time.
comment_workload: It must take a lot of time for a beginner to understand the content. Since he releases the PowerPoint just before the lecture, there is no time for me to prepare, and I end up confused during the entire lecture.

author: st4ll
comment_content:
An introduction course to programming language theories taught in Scala 3. You are expected to get familiar with basic Functional Programming concepts and tricks, subtyping and generics, basic structural proofs for immutable data structures, lazy data structures, and etc. You will need to implement a simpliefied Scheme interpreter from skeleton codes.

The course also quickly goes over the topics of Logic Programming, Asynchronous Programming, and Actor Systems.

This course is interesting if you want to learn about programming language theories. You may refer to the book *Types and Programming Languages* by Benjamin C. Pierce as a complement of the course materials, or if you want to learn the course without taking it.
comment_teaching:
The professor is nice and passionate about the contents he teaches.
The two TAs are helpful and speak fluent English.

The teaching team hosts a Discord server where you can discuss the course contents with the professor/TAs/classmates, as well as ask for hints for assignments. The TAs give timely and helpful responses.
comment_grading:
I reached the upper quartile scores for 3 PAs.

I got median scores for both midterm and final.

I received a B.
comment_workload:
3 Programming Assignments + Midterm + Final

Moderate level of coding, testing and debugging.

As for midterm/final, the provided past papers and practice problems are very helpful for your preparation.

author: Re
comment_content:
Well this course is a hard course which serves as an introduction course of functional programming, along with an introduction of Scala programming, introduction to some abstract algebra, introduction to a functional solution to some common problems in computer science such as reactive programming, design patterns and etc., as well as interpretation on Lisp.

This is a hard course as a whole. If you want to know more, consider reading other reviews written for this semester or last Fall semester.
comment_teaching:
Not really good. Apart from the fact that the TAs and professor are willing to answers questions. The professor seems does not know how to pace his teaching. Given the content of this course is hard, he tend to finish the lectures in 80 minutes in a 110 minutes lesson by reading his slides.

The PA, Labs and exercises are not quite able to cover most of the stuff in course. The preparation materials of the exam are far from enough for you to be well prepared
comment_grading:
10%*3 Programming Assignments
30% Midterm
40% Final
comment_workload: This is a hard course which it should take immense amount of time to at least understand what is going on in each slides.

author: ruozhilaji
title: Not that great !!!
comment_content:
SML, Flex & Bison, Prolog, CUDA, and some other minor topics.
The content is a little bit old but the ideas are important.
comment_teaching:
I do not think her teaching is good. She speaks quite slowly and makes me really sleepy. So I usually watch the recording at double speed.

For office hours, I do not make use of it since it is just after the class. I belieave there are lots of students including me having other classes after this course.

It seems the Prof does not prefer to answer questions via emails. She usually replies to my emails 2~4 days after I send them to her. Also one of the TAs is not reachable, he never replies to my emails. The other is relatively friendly but not helpful. It seems that she does not know too much about the course materials and cannot answer my questions.

I think the Prof does not care about this year's feedback since she will go to the GZ campus in the next academic year with a high probability. So she may just treat the teaching as one of the compulsory tasks.
comment_grading:
I think the grading is much worse compared with previous offerings. I was tricked by previous reviews. This year, the mean (approximately lower than 75%) seems to be B-.

From my understanding, this is unfair this year. If the Prof wants absolute grading, she should make sure the overall means are relatively stable.
comment_workload: Assignment 3 is a little bit tricky this year. And I think all the assignments are more time-consuming this year. You'd better leave one afternoon for each.

author: hello
comment_content: Covers functional programming in Standard ML (SML), language parsing in FLEX and BISON, logic programming in PROLOG, and GPU parallel programming in CUDA. The concepts about programming languages include language constructs, grammar representation, program translation and interpretation, storage allocation and run-time organization.
comment_teaching:
Professor is very kind and I love her teaching style. She teaches a bit slow and explains everything in detail. She also encourages student participation in class and asks for students to stop her at anytime if there are questions.

She didn't rush any topics, and will end class early if she finishes covering a topic because she doesn't want to start a new topic with only a few minutes left in class.

She also hosted a review session for midterm and final, which I found very useful.

TAs are also very helpful, and the labs are useful. I constantly made appointments with the TA to make sure I understood all the concepts.
comment_grading:
Professor said she did absolute grading (means no curve).

30% 3 Assignments (10% each)
30% Midterm
40% Final
comment_workload: There were only three assignments, and you're given about 1-1.5 weeks to complete it. Assignments were doable in the time frame, and professor encourages students to go to her or TAs if they need help.

author: Yuken
comment_content:
Content focuses mainly on using Scala as a tool for learning about functional programming and some of the theories used in understanding this paradigm. Other paradigms and languages are touched on near the end of the semester, like Prolog and Lisp, but it is still very much connected to Scala and how to implement this with Scala.
The very last topics (Actors, ...) are hard to understand and taught through very fast, but they are also not tested.
comment_teaching: The teaching is adequate and the prof will answer any questions if you have them. There is also an official discord where the prof, TAs and peers will help in answering questions and homework.
comment_grading: Grading is ok, with most of it being on the exams and 30% on the assignments.
comment_workload:
There is almost a lab every week, but the work or attendance is not graded so just treat it like an useful reviewer.
There are 3 programming assignments that have about 2 weeks for each, and the assignments are not hard to the point they require all those days to work on them. A lot of guidance is given in the instructions for the assignments and you can also just ask for help in the discord.
There is a midterm and a final, both openbook. Since there are past papers given on canvas you can get a good feeling of what would be tested and it would not be hard. This is especially true for the final because the hardest parts to understand/implement are not tested, so just referencing past papers' ranges is a great way to review and wouldnt be too heavy.

author: Richard Blažek
title: Good course
comment_content:
The course is largely inspired by the book SICP (Structure and Interpretation of Computer Program) and the eponymous course at MIT. It uses Scala instead of Lisp, which I think isn't a very good choice, since Scala is much more convoluted language with a lot of syntactic quirks, but it may be easier to learn for people used to Java/C++.

If you have prior knowledge of SICP, it's really easy. If you don't, that's still ok, because Prof. Parreaux goes quite in detail in explaining all the necessary concepts.

Given that the topics covered in the course are quite foundational in computer science, it's probably a good idea to take this course to learn them.

Topics covered include: Scala, functional programming, higher-order functions, referential transparency, currying, logic programming, concurrency, object-oriented programming, type system (generics and covariance/invariance) and writing a Lisp interpreter.
comment_teaching:
Prof. Parreaux is really good, he explains things in detail. All lectures are recorded.

Speaks good English with a slight French accent. As a proper Frenchman, he has a moustache.
comment_grading:
Three programming assignments 10% each
Midterm examination 30%
Final examination 40%
Lab exercises aren't graded

There are practice exams from the past years
comment_workload:
The assignments require some effort. For the exams, you don't have to memorise any material (you can print a cheat sheet), but you do need to understand it.

Btw, everybody signs an attendance sheet in the labs, but when I asked the TA in person about attendance, he told me that they actually don't do anything with the attendance sheets, so it's not a problem if you skip the labs.

author: AlexJ
title: Great Intro to PL Class
comment_content: A comprehensive overview of PL topics, with particular focus on FP. Would have liked some more overview of non-FP paradigms, possibly spending more time on Lisp itself too rather than Scala, but it still helped a lot.
comment_teaching: Concise lectures that grasped the most important points of each topic. Great to ask questions and get clarification on important concepts, but you need to keep up with each lecture to understand the last.
comment_grading: Fair on exam and homework grades
comment_workload: HW load was relatively light, and if you understand the lab sessions the exams are very doable.

author: arod920
comment_content:
Goes over non-trending programming languages: SML for functional programming, FLEX and BISON to learn about lexers and parsers, and PROLOG for logical programming.
Its a brief overview of each language without too much in-depth details and concepts.
Though it was interesting learning new languages, it could have been better with more detail.
Also briefly covered concurrent programming and GPU programming with CUDA-C with time leftover.
Concurrent programming was not covered in the exam this time, but has been in the past.
comment_teaching:
Prof. Luo is extremely kind, speaks good English, and very accommodating to students.
She goes over the concepts at a pretty steady and comfortable pace, and is never hesitant to answer any questions.
comment_grading:
Not bad, I would say.
3 assignments account for 10% each, midterm 30%, final 40%.
Final exam is cumulative, so quite a bit to review and can be sort of cumbersome having to switch between languages in one paper.
I don't know if the grades were given on an absolute or comparative scale, but sufficient work gets you a satisfactory grade, I guess.
comment_workload:
I didn't feel too burdened with the course workload. The slides themselves are very short, but practice on one's own initiative is important for this course.
There are 3 assignments, which are not very time-consuming.

author: Bibubibu
title: Frustrating EVERYTHING
comment_content:
99% Scala 3,
1% - Lisp, Prolog, Async Programming
HIDDEN pre-requisite: MATH 3121 Abstract Algebra

The entire course revolves around Scala 3 and wide range of concepts in abstract algebra.
Functional programming itself exhibits λ-calculus and very inspiring ways to model solutions. But this course's curriculum obviously disregards the importance of "knowledge transfer". And it is COMPLETELY DIFFERENT from the course description as well, while I expected to get a touch of different languages. And it's disappointing and depressing.

NOTHING ON THE COURSE DESCRIPTION WAS TAUGHT. AND NOTHING TAUGHT IS ON THE COURSE DESCRIPTION.
comment_teaching:
Professor likes to put simple concepts into abstract, ridiculously entangled theories. It might bring pleasure to him trying to complicate things. I don't know.

No one goes to lecture because he's just reading from the slides. Skipping all the abstract concepts. It doesn't convince me that he can transfer any useful knowledge to me.
comment_grading:
Haven't released. I have depressive and super negative thoughts trying to keep up with the pace, even though my ability is adequate to kept up with exp. 1st Hon.

Mid term's full mark was lowered by 25% because, "Too many students would receive a low grade so we did the adjustment".
comment_workload:
Rather light, which is probably not a good thing because you are likely to lose the pace with the course.

Course content itself is not comparable to 4000-level course.

You will find yourself hard to digest each of the chapters. It's likely that you sit here a whole day, searching here and there on the internet, while still falling to understand a single slide.
Honestly I think this is 5000-level course, with at least 5-cred workload in studying.

title: Great Introduction to Functional Programming and Scala
comment_content:
The content is very rich compared to other courses, giving you a nice introduction to functional programming. I'd say after taking the course, you kind of learn another way of looking at this world, another kind of logic thinking which is useful, making solving many problems much easier.
The first half of the course is mainly giving you an insight to functional programming with Scala while the second half of the course introduces other programming paradigms such as reactive programming, logic programming and more.
After finishing the course, you'll also be able to write code in a more concise and clear manner, with much less repetition of code and be able to write code in more modular way and not have dependencies, code tangled around.
comment_teaching: He knows a lot about Scala and could really tell why functional programming is good for many usages, better than imperative programming in many cases. And by the way, C++ is an unreasonable language, ugly to write for many use cases.
comment_grading:
Programming Assignments: 10% each x3
Midterm: 30%
Final Exam: 40%
Can get somewhat a nice normal distribution for the gradings. You won't need to worry about having too much people getting marks too high and the A, B, C ranges all get stuck above 90.
comment_workload: Mandatory attendence for lab sessions. Workload is fair, only 3 programming assignments throughout the whole semester. Just join the lectures and go through the notes once after the lectures would be ok. The concepts and things are explained quite clear.

author: blairC
comment_content:
the programming language used: scala
Contents of this course had changed from this semester so there aren't many past study materials
This course is about functional programming language...
very hard...
don't take this course unless you are a genius
comment_teaching: ok... he is kind and willing to help students
comment_grading:
I think the whole grading system was okay...
Level of difficulty of Final and Midterm: Adequate
comment_workload: can't say the workload is high compare to other comp courses, but you have to study a lot to keep up with the contents.
